skill.name: azure-communication-callingserver-java
skill.description: Guidance for maintaining legacy Azure Communication Services CallingServer Java SDK code and planning migration to Call Automation.
skill.summary: Maintain deprecated Azure CallingServer Java code and compare it with Call Automation APIs.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported command execution and weak cryptography patterns, but review found these are false positives in markdown documentation and metadata text. No prompt injection, malicious behavior, network exfiltration, filesystem access, or executable script content was found in the scanned file. The main residual concern is that the skill documents a deprecated SDK, so users need clear migration guidance.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are markdown inline code, fenced code blocks, and table entries. They document dependency names, class names, and Java examples rather than executable shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are visibly markdown formatting and Java documentation examples. No shell command, command interpreter, or user-input execution path is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is a Metadata False Positive
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography alert points to the skill description line. That line describes Azure Communication Services and does not name or configure a cryptographic algorithm.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 contains descriptive metadata about a deprecated SDK. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak algorithm usage.

low_findings.title: Deprecated SDK Guidance Requires User Awareness
low_findings.description: The skill is focused on a deprecated Azure Communication Services CallingServer Java SDK. This is not malicious, but publishing should make the maintenance-only scope clear.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deprecation warning is explicit in the heading and usage guidance. The risk is operational rather than a confirmed security vulnerability.

content.user_title: Maintain Legacy Azure CallingServer Java Code
content.value_statement: Legacy CallingServer Java projects need careful maintenance because the SDK is deprecated. This skill helps compare old APIs with Call Automation replacements.
actual_capabilities: Identify the deprecated CallingServer Java dependency and its Call Automation replacement.
Map legacy client classes to current Call Automation class names.
Show legacy client creation beside the newer client pattern.
Explain legacy call recording methods and the newer migration direction.
Suggest trigger phrases for maintenance and migration work.
limitations: It does not provide complete Call Automation implementation guidance.
It is intended for legacy maintenance, not new Azure calling projects.
It does not verify current Azure SDK versions at runtime.
It does not include runnable tests or project scaffolding.

use_cases.title: Review legacy calling dependencies
use_cases.description: Check an older Java service for deprecated CallingServer package usage before planning updates.

use_cases.title: Plan a migration path
use_cases.description: Compare old CallingServer client names with Call Automation names during migration planning.

use_cases.title: Document support boundaries
use_cases.description: Create clear notes that explain why new work should use Call Automation instead.

prompt_templates.title: Identify legacy usage
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a Java dependency file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Check this Java project for Azure CallingServer dependency usage and summarize whether it is legacy-only.

prompt_templates.title: Compare client classes
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate code review during migration planning.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these CallingServer classes with their Call Automation replacements and explain the expected migration impact.

prompt_templates.title: Review recording flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this legacy CallingServer recording flow and list the concepts I must verify before migrating it.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare migration guidance
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for a service migration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a concise migration checklist from CallingServer Java to Call Automation for this service design.

output_examples.input: A Java service still depends on the CallingServer package.
output_examples.output: The service uses a deprecated Azure Communication Services SDK. Keep changes limited to maintenance and plan migration to Call Automation.

output_examples.input: A maintainer asks which client builder replaces the legacy builder.
output_examples.output: Use the Call Automation client builder for new work. Treat the old CallingServer builder as legacy support only.

output_examples.input: A team wants guidance for call recording migration.
output_examples.output: Review the current recording start, pause, resume, and stop flows. Then map them to Call Automation documentation before implementation.

best_practices: Use this skill only when maintaining existing CallingServer Java code.
Confirm current Azure SDK versions from official Azure documentation before implementation.
Plan new features against Call Automation instead of extending deprecated APIs.
anti_patterns: Starting a new project with the deprecated CallingServer dependency.
Assuming class name mapping is a complete migration plan.
Changing production call flows without validating Azure service behavior.

faq.question: Is this skill for new Azure Communication Services projects?
faq.answer: No. It is for legacy CallingServer Java maintenance only.

faq.question: What should new projects use instead?
faq.answer: New projects should use Azure Communication Services Call Automation.

faq.question: Does this skill execute shell commands?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed skill contains markdown documentation and Java examples only.

faq.question: Can it complete a full migration automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides comparison guidance, but migration still requires code review and testing.

faq.question: Does it cover call recording concepts?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows legacy recording operations and points users toward Call Automation guidance.

faq.question: Should I verify SDK versions separately?
faq.answer: Yes. Check official Azure documentation before making implementation decisions.
